method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The island’s Japanese name is Hashima, written 端島.[2]  
It is widely known by the nickname Gunkanjima, written 軍艦島, which is commonly rendered in English as “Battleship Island.”[2]  
The nickname derives from the island’s appearance from a distance; contemporary observers compared its silhouette to a warship, particularly the Japanese battleship Tosa.[2][4]  
The association was noted in the press by 1916, when the Osaka Asahi Shimbun reported that Hashima resembled a giant battleship with two chimneys, and the name Gunkanjima was coined by the Nagasaki Nichinichi Shimbun in 1921.[4]

==History==
Coal use in the area predated Hashima’s industrial development, as residents of nearby Takashima were said to have collected coal from exposed beds for household fuel.[2] Hashima itself was initially associated with local fishing and small-scale coal mining before it was transformed into a dense undersea coal-mining settlement.[1][3] Over time, the island was enlarged by landfill and protected by seawalls, while its built environment was organized as a Mitsubishi company town that concentrated labor, housing, provisioning, and social life within a restricted area.[4]

By 1916, Hashima was producing about 150,000 tons of coal annually and had a population of more than 3,000.[2] That year Mitsubishi built a large reinforced-concrete apartment block on the island to address overcrowding and typhoon damage, a structure described as Japan’s first concrete building of significant size.[2] During the early twentieth century, additional concrete apartment blocks, schools, medical facilities, and recreational amenities were built to support the growing mining community.[5] The island’s dense concrete landscape also reflected experimentation in modern construction and the practical need to withstand fires, storms, winds, and waves.[1]

Hashima’s history was closely tied to Japan’s modernization and changing energy policies from the Meiji period through the twentieth century.[2] At its height in 1959, the island’s population density reportedly reached about 83,500 people per square kilometre, with the residential district approaching 139,100 people per square kilometre.[3] By the 1950s, more than 5,000 people lived on the island’s roughly 16 acres, making it one of the most densely populated places in the world.[5] Community life in the postwar decades later produced memories of schools, festivals, rooftop play, and close neighborly relations, although such recollections could obscure earlier coercive labor histories.[4]

During Japan’s wartime mobilization, Hashima formed part of a broader imperial labor regime involving coercive recruitment, policing, confinement, wage discipline, and unequal ability to leave, especially for workers marked by colonial status.[4] The island is therefore remembered not only as an industrial and architectural site but also as a place connected to forced labor and the harsh conditions of wartime extraction.[3][4]

Hashima declined after Japan’s coal industry weakened in the 1960s as petroleum became the dominant energy source.[5] Mitsubishi closed the mines in 1974, and the island’s residents departed, leaving behind apartment blocks, schools, shops, and other facilities.[5] After abandonment, the buildings deteriorated under exposure to the elements, turning the former mining settlement into a prominent industrial ruin.[1][5] Its later inclusion among UNESCO-listed industrial heritage sites has been accompanied by diplomatic tension and debate over how its history, including wartime labor, should be represented.[1][3][4]

==Current status==
The island is now uninhabited, with empty high-rise blocks, seawalls, and other built remains exposed to wind and salt spray.[5] Contemporary travel writing presents it as an abandoned industrial-heritage destination and an example of urban-exploration tourism, attracting interest from photographers, historians, and visitors seeking non-mainstream heritage sites.[2] Its ruins have also become a subject of academic and artistic work, including the AHRC-supported project “Future of Ruins: Reclaiming Abandonment and Toxicity on Hashima Island” and the related 2013 performance “Return to Battleship Island”.[3]

Public representations of the island increasingly include fictional and digital versions, with accounts noting its association with *Skyfall* and survival-horror games as well as tourism and heritage imagery of “picturesque decay”.[4] A survey cited in 2025 identified eleven publicly accessible immersive or virtual-reality projects focused on Hashima, ranging from early stereoscopic prototypes to museum installations, university scanning projects, VRChat worlds, and commercial walking simulators.[4] The same account argued that such projects often frame the site through selective narratives, making Hashima a case study in the difficulties of representing contested heritage through immersive media.[4]

Disputes over the interpretation of Hashima’s wartime labor history remain active in international heritage discussions.[1] At the 47th session of the UNESCO World Heritage Committee in Paris, South Korea requested renewed examination of Japan’s follow-up measures concerning the site, while Japan argued that it had implemented earlier resolutions.[1] After the committee failed to reach consensus, an anonymous ballot among the 21 member states adopted Japan’s revised proposal by seven votes to three, with several abstentions.[1] The committee’s outcome favored addressing the dispute through bilateral dialogue rather than reopening it through UNESCO procedures, making further formal discussion during that session unlikely.[1]

==Access==
Public access to Hashima is permitted only through authorized guided boat tours, most of which depart from Nagasaki Port.[1][2] Nagasaki Port can be reached from Nagasaki Eki-mae by tram to Ohato, or from Nagasaki Airport by bus to Ohato.[1] Landing is conditional on sea and weather conditions; Nagasaki City regulations require waves below 0.5 m, wind speeds below 5 m/s, and visibility above 500 m before sightseeing boats may berth.[1] Even when a tour departs, the captain may cancel landing if conditions are judged unsafe.[1][2] Because of these restrictions, boats are reported to land on the island on only about 100 days per year.[1]

Visitors who land are kept to designated viewing routes rather than being allowed to enter or roam freely among the island’s structures.[2][4] Tours are conducted in guided groups, and visitor movement is controlled to reduce crowding and protect the site.[4] On busy days, visitor numbers have exceeded 1,400 per day, with around 260 visitors per hour passing through the route system.[4]

==In popular culture==
Hashima Island has become prominent in popular culture through images of its abandoned urban landscape, with some commentators arguing that it is now better known through fictional and digital representations than through archival accounts of the site.[2] One of its best-known screen associations is the James Bond film *Skyfall* (2012), in which audiences have identified the island with the villain Raoul Silva’s lair.[2][4] National Geographic also filmed on the island for the documentary series *One Strange Rock*, further circulating its image as a spectacular ruin in global media.[4]

Scholars have criticized some of these representations, particularly after *Skyfall*, for aestheticizing the ruins in ways that can obscure debates over historical injustice and accountability.[1] Critical media studies of Hashima argue that popular images of the island often transform a contested industrial and wartime site into a setting for entertainment, tourism, or picturesque decay.[1][2] Tourism campaigns and heritage publicity have similarly helped circulate the ruins as visually striking urban decay.[2]

Hashima has also appeared in digital and interactive media, including survival-horror games such as *The Closed Ward* (2018) and related works.[2] A survey of immersive and virtual-reality projects about the island identified eleven publicly accessible projects over nearly two decades, ranging from early stereoscopic prototypes and museum installations to university scanning projects, VRChat worlds, and commercial walking simulators.[2] Such projects have been discussed in relation to authenticity, ethical game design, pedagogy, and the representation of contested heritage.[2]

==World Heritage Site approval controversy==
Hashima was included in Japan’s 2015 UNESCO World Heritage inscription for the “Sites of Japan’s Meiji Industrial Revolution,” a serial property comprising 23 old industrial facilities intended to illustrate Japan’s rapid emergence as an industrial power.[2][3] Japan’s nomination emphasized the period from 1850 to 1910, while critics argued that the presentation risked excluding later wartime experiences connected to Korean labor at some of the same sites.[1][3] South Korea opposed the bid on the grounds that seven nominated locations were associated with forced labor during World War II and should not be presented only as symbols of Japanese modernization.[1]

Hashima was one of the most disputed locations, with South Korean critics stating that about 600 Koreans worked in its undersea coal mines and that many were believed to have died from exposure or overwork.[1] Korean and Japanese diplomats held negotiations in Tokyo over how the circumstances of Korean workers would be represented in connection with the nomination.[1] Kim Min-chul of Kyung Hee University argued that the seven forced-labor-related sites were central to the nomination and that Japan regarded them as “glorious sites,” illustrating the disagreement over historical interpretation.[1]

After the inscription, controversy continued over whether Japan’s interpretation of the site adequately addressed Koreans brought from the former Korean Peninsula.[3] In 2020, shortly after the IHIC opened to the public, South Korean Foreign Minister Kang Kyung-wha wrote to UNESCO Director-General Audrey Azoulay that its explanations concerning people from the former Korean Peninsula were insufficient and asked UNESCO to consider removing Hashima’s World Heritage status.[3] IHIC managing director Koko Kato responded that the center had gathered more than 100,000 primary materials, including testimony from former Hashima residents, and said UNESCO should judge the matter using what she described as accurate information.[3]

The dispute has also drawn criticism from Japanese scholars who argue that Hashima should be interpreted as a site for historical reflection rather than celebration alone.[5] Takazane Yasunori of Nagasaki University compared such an approach to World Heritage sites associated with Auschwitz, Hiroshima, and the slave trade, arguing that Hashima should provide a warning about events that should not be repeated.[5] Commentators in South Korea have similarly argued that UNESCO recognition itself is not inherently objectionable, but that the site’s classification and explanation require a fuller account of the wartime labor issue.[5]
